replies.tweet_snippet: Meet Hermes Agent, the open source agent that grows with you — multi-level persistent memory, dedicated remote terminal access, and collaborative growth built in.
replies.drafted_reply: persistent memory gets interesting once it survives real workflow turbulence. remembering chat history is easy; preserving state across tool failures, approvals, and handoffs is the part that makes agents actually usable.
replies.why_this_tweet: High-signal launch in agent infrastructure with a concrete angle on memory and reliability that matches the project thesis around orchestration.

replies.tweet_snippet: My 2026 AI predictions just dropped. Context Engineering and Memory — the race to help AI remember you, your work, and your preferences is the defining battleground.
replies.drafted_reply: context engineering is starting to look more like systems design than prompting. the hard problem is deciding what graduates from transient context into durable memory, and what should be forgotten on purpose.
replies.why_this_tweet: Strong fit for a thoughtful technical reply on memory and orchestration, while broadening the set beyond pure agent-launch discourse.

replies.tweet_snippet: Yes, AI Continues To Make Rapid Progress, Including Towards AGI — here's the evidence people are overlooking.
replies.drafted_reply: slight pushback: the underrated signal is not just capability progress, it’s coordination progress. once agents can chain tools reliably with memory and guardrails, users experience "wow, this works" well before the benchmark arguments are settled.
replies.why_this_tweet: Lets us add a differentiated take on where practical progress is showing up without repeating the source tweet’s framing.

replies.tweet_snippet: 5 million views. twitter has really fried our brains. I was off of twitter all day yesterday — didn't realize this had gone viral until I got a text. It was surreal opening it back up and noticing how fake and performative all of it seems.
replies.drafted_reply: the dangerous part for founders is that the platform rewards narrating momentum more than proving it. how do you keep build-in-public honest once the algorithm pays more for performance than evidence?
replies.why_this_tweet: Different niche from the AI posts and a strong opportunity to ask a pointed founder question instead of piling on with agreement.
